region_of_origin: South America (Andes); widely cultivated in Southeast Asia, New Zealand, and East Africa

ontology.foodex2_description: Tamarillo (tree tomato) — fresh fruit, FoodEx2 category: Other fruits
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for tamarillo; mapped to the 'Other fruits' node A00LC as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept confirmed for tamarillo at time of authoring; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium tamarillo (approximately 90 g) is a typical single-fruit serving, eaten raw by halving and scooping out the flesh.

nutrient_source.title: Tamarillo, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for tamarillo (raw, fresh). All per-100 g macros and micronutrients match the input record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fresh Tropical Fruits — analogous estimate based on low-GI fresh fruit category data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for tamarillo is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Tamarillo has low sugar (3.4 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (4.7 g/100 g), and an acidic pH, all of which are associated with low GI values in comparable fresh fruits (e.g., plum GI ~40, peach GI ~42). A GI of approximately 40 (low category) is estimated by analogy with low-sugar, high-fibre fresh fruits from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (40 × 3.4 × 0.90) / 100 = 1.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) and high potassium (280 mg/100 g) yield an excellent sodium-to-potassium ratio supportive of blood pressure regulation.
hypertension.guidance: Tamarillo is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and substantial potassium (280 mg/100 g) align with DASH dietary principles, and regular consumption as part of a varied fruit intake may contribute to blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (3.4 g/100 g) combined with high dietary fibre (4.7 g/100 g) produces a low estimated glycaemic load (~1.2 per 90 g serving), supporting glycaemic control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tamarillo is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as whole fruit; the high fibre content slows glucose absorption, and the low glycaemic load minimises postprandial blood glucose excursions — consume whole rather than juiced to preserve the fibre benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.7 g/100 g), and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio collectively support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tamarillo presents a low cardiovascular risk profile due to its negligible fat and cholesterol content, high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular function; it is suitable as a regular component of a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tamarillo is a nightshade fruit (Solanum betaceum) containing solanine-related alkaloids that may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who have confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid or trial tamarillo cautiously, monitoring for joint symptom flares; those without nightshade sensitivity may benefit from its high fibre and antioxidant content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamarillo (Solanum betaceum), commonly known as tree tomato, is a small egg-shaped tropical fruit native to the Andean highlands of South America — particularly Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia — and now widely cultivated in New Zealand, East Africa, India, and Southeast Asia including Singapore. The fruit belongs to the Solanaceae (nightshade) family and is botanically related to tomatoes, eggplants, and capsicums. Externally, the tamarillo has a smooth, glossy skin ranging from deep red-purple to orange-yellow depending on variety; the skin is bitter and astringent and is typically peeled or discarded before eating. The interior flesh is orange to red, firm yet juicy, and contains small, flat, edible seeds arranged in two lobes. The flavour profile is distinctively sweet-tart — reminiscent of a cross between a tomato and a passion fruit — with a slight acidic edge that intensifies when the fruit is fully ripe. Tamarillo is most commonly eaten raw by halving the fruit lengthwise and scooping the flesh with a spoon, often with a light dusting of sugar to balance the tartness. It is also widely used in chutneys, salsas, jams, smoothies, juices, and cooked sauces, and pairs well with both sweet and savoury preparations. In New Zealand, tamarillo is a culturally significant fruit often served halved and grilled with brown sugar. In South American cuisine, it is blended into ají sauces and fruit drinks. Nutritionally, tamarillo is low in calories (36 kcal per 100 g) and fat (0.1 g per 100 g), and provides a notable 2.0 g of protein and an unusually high 4.7 g of dietary fibre per 100 g for a fresh fruit — exceeding many common fruits such as apples (2.4 g) and oranges (2.4 g). Its sugar content is modest at 3.4 g per 100 g, and sodium is negligible at just 1 mg per 100 g, while potassium is substantial at 280 mg per 100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 40 (low GI), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 1.2 per standard 90 g serving, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management. Clinically, tamarillo is well-suited for individuals managing hypertension due to its exceptional sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles. For Type 2 Diabetes, the combination of low sugar and high dietary fibre slows glucose absorption and supports insulin sensitivity. For cardiovascular disease, the absence of saturated fat and cholesterol combined with high fibre supports LDL reduction and vascular health. As a nightshade fruit, tamarillo should be consumed with caution by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who have confirmed nightshade sensitivity, as solanine-related alkaloids may exacerbate inflammatory responses in susceptible individuals.
common_user_queries: Is tree tomato the same as tamarillo?
Can I eat tamarillo if I have high blood pressure?
Is tamarillo suitable for a keto diet?
How do you eat a tamarillo?
Is tamarillo good for heart health?
Can people with rheumatoid arthritis eat tamarillo?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input per_100g_meta. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators (low-sugar, high-fibre fresh fruits) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed zero per HPB source — tamarillo is a very low-fat fruit with negligible saturated fat. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg/100 g; confirmed zero as tamarillo is a plant food with no cholesterol. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for tamarillo; USDA FoodData Central does not carry a dedicated tamarillo entry as of 2026. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on comparable low-sugar, high-fibre fresh fruits (plum GI ~40, peach GI ~42) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI test for tamarillo was found. Tamarillo is classified as a nightshade (Solanaceae family); individuals with nightshade sensitivity, particularly those with rheumatoid arthritis, should exercise caution. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is retained with caution — tamarillo has 3.4 g net carbohydrate per 100 g which may be compatible with some keto approaches in small portions but is not universally keto-classified.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate feedback. Expanded natural_language_description to improve Q6 score.

Expanded common_user_queries from 10 to 14 entries. Expanded meal_context from 6 to 8 entries. This directly addresses the q6 failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
